name: Text Processing Pipeline with sed, awk, cut, and sort
description: Build text processing pipelines to transform, extract, and aggregate data from CSVs, TSVs, and structured text files using standard Unix tools.
trendingReason: CLI text processing remains the fastest way to transform data without writing a full program

steps.title: Extract columns from CSV/TSV data
steps.description: Use cut or awk to extract specific columns from delimited files.
steps.tip: Use awk -F',' '{print $1, $3}' for more control over output formatting and delimiters.

steps.title: Transform text with sed substitutions
steps.description: Use sed to find and replace patterns, remove lines, or reformat text.
steps.tip: Use -E for extended regex (no need to escape parentheses). Use -i.bak to edit in place with a backup.

steps.title: Sort and deduplicate data
steps.description: Sort by specific columns and remove duplicate entries.
steps.tip: -t sets the delimiter, -k2,2 sorts by column 2, -k1,1n sorts column 1 numerically.

steps.title: Aggregate and compute with awk
steps.description: Calculate sums, averages, and counts from columnar data.
steps.command:
awk -F',' 'NR>1 {sum+=$3; count++} END {printf "Total: %.2f
Average: %.2f
Count: %d
", sum, sum/count, count}' sales.csv

steps.title: Build a multi-stage pipeline
steps.description: Chain tools together to filter, transform, and summarize data in one command.
steps.command: cat access.log | awk '{print $1}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10
steps.tip: Read pipelines left to right: extract IPs, sort them, count unique occurrences, sort by count descending, show top 10.

fullScript:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

FILE=${1:?'Usage: $0 <data-file>'}

[[ ! -f "$FILE" ]] && { echo "File not found: $FILE" >&2; exit 1; }

# Detect delimiter
FIRST_LINE=$(head -1 "$FILE")
if [[ "$FIRST_LINE" == *$'\t'* ]]; then
  DELIM=$'\t'
  echo "Detected: TSV"
elif [[ "$FIRST_LINE" == *','* ]]; then
  DELIM=','
  echo "Detected: CSV"
else
  DELIM=' '
  echo "Detected: space-delimited"
fi

echo ""
echo "=== File Stats ==="
echo "Lines: $(wc -l < "$FILE")"
echo "Columns: $(head -1 "$FILE" | awk -F"$DELIM" '{print NF}')"
echo "Header: $(head -1 "$FILE")"

echo ""
echo "=== First 5 rows ==="
head -6 "$FILE" | column -t -s"$DELIM"

echo ""
echo "=== Unique values in column 1 ==="
tail -n +2 "$FILE" | cut -d"$DELIM" -f1 | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10

echo ""
echo "=== Common Pipelines ==="
echo "Top values:    cut -d',' -f1 file | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn"
echo "Filter rows:   awk -F',' '\$3 > 100' file"
echo "Replace text:  sed 's/old/new/g' file"
echo "Column stats:  awk -F',' '{sum+=\$2} END{print sum}' file"

faq.question: How do I handle CSV files with quoted fields containing commas?
faq.answer: Standard cut and awk do not handle quoted CSV fields correctly. Use csvtool, miller (mlr), or Python's csv module for proper RFC 4180 CSV parsing. Example: mlr --csv filter '$amount > 100' data.csv.

faq.question: What is the difference between sort -n and sort -V?
faq.answer: -n sorts numerically (1, 2, 10, 20). -V sorts by version number (1.2.3, 1.10.0). Without -n, sort orders lexicographically (1, 10, 2, 20). Always use -n for numeric data.

faq.question: How do I process very large files efficiently?
faq.answer: Use LC_ALL=C for faster locale-independent processing. Pipe through tools instead of loading into memory. Use split to break large files into chunks for parallel processing. For files over 10GB, consider using mawk instead of gawk for 2-5x speed improvement.
